deconstruction_targets: the opinion that only incorruptible things are subject to Divine providence, and that individuals of corruptible species are not individually subject to divine governance
the view that denies individual particularity in divine providence for all things, especially lower creatures and humans
constructive_proposals: all things are subject to Divine providence in both general and particular senses
God immediately provides for all things and ordains them to some end according to His causality
the providence of God is the reason or cause by which all things are ordered to a purpose
God's knowledge extends to individual things, like a craftsman knows his work
evil and witchcraft occur through Divine permission as part of a greater good, not as violations of divine care
moral_justification: God permits evil and witchcraft not out of indifference but as necessary components for the greater good, since He can extract good from evil, just as martyrdom arises from persecution and faith is proven through trials.
summary: The text engages in a mixed rhetorical mode, deconstructing medieval scholastic views that limit divine providence to universal or incorruptible entities while constructively arguing for a comprehensive, individual scope of God's providential care over all creation. It maintains high moral intensity by framing divine permission of evil as necessary and merciful, using scriptural and philosophical reasoning without endorsing destructive condemnation.
